moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with the unusual 1.h4, known as the Kádas Opening—a rare but principled flank attack that immediately stakes a claim on the kingside. Rather than contesting central control right away, we're preparing to control g5 and later support an e4 advance with our h-pawn. This move is unconventional enough to throw unprepared opponents off-balance while maintaining flexibility in our setup.

Black immediately counters by striking at our center with 1...f5, preparing to challenge our e4 advance and claim space in the middle. This is a natural response to our flank move, and it commits Black to an aggressive, pawn-forward setup rather than developing pieces first.

Now we strike at the center with 2.e4, and this is the critical moment of the Steinbok Gambit—we're offering the e4 pawn as bait to open lines and seize the initiative. By offering this pawn, we hope to expose Black's king and create attacking chances before they can consolidate their advanced f5-pawn.

Black accepts the gambit by capturing 2...fxe4, which is the main line and most natural response. Taking the pawn advances Black's kingside but also weakens the e5 square and opens the f-file—both of which we can exploit in the coming moves.

With 3.d3, we attack the e4 pawn while keeping our options open, threatening to recapture it while preparing to develop our light-squared bishop and potentially reroute pieces. This move also supports a future f4 push and keeps control of the central dark squares, maintaining the tactical tension we've created with our gambit.
